frameworks.name: computational cognitive process models
frameworks.one_line: model families accounting for multi-alternative multi-attribute choice probabilities

frameworks.name: utility-based rational choice theory
frameworks.one_line: invariance assumptions violated by observed preference reversals

frameworks.name: context effects (similarity, attraction, compromise)
frameworks.one_line: benchmark phenomena used to differentiate competing process models

core_question: How can paradigms producing similarity, attraction, and compromise effects be presented coherently so computational process models of multi-alternative choice can be compared?
key_assumptions: choice probabilities depend on local context (the set under consideration)
preference reversals reveal violations of invariance in rational choice
the three context effects index theoretically important phenomena
manipulations: addition of a third decoy option to a two-alternative set
varying stimulus arrangement across similarity, attraction, compromise paradigms
key_findings: adding a decoy systematically shifts relative choice probabilities between two alternatives
stimulus arrangements producing the three effects vary substantially across studies
interpretations: context effects demonstrate that multi-alternative choice is contingent on the choice set, not invariant
varying paradigms make cross-study comparison of process model accounts challenging without a unified framing
limitations_noted: heterogeneity of stimulus arrangements complicates direct model comparison
